packTitle: Preparedness, Kits, and Home Readiness Import Batch
notes: This batch normalizes Perplexity research into Replit-ready guide objects.
Guide types have been normalized to action_card, scenario_guide, preparedness_guide, or reference_guide.
Most items in this batch are net-new preparedness guides rather than upgrades to existing core survival topics.
The content is optimized for short card-style presentation with expandable detail underneath.

categories.summary: Preparedness, kits, communication planning, shelter-in-place, medical backup, pets, and home readiness guides normalized from Perplexity research.

guides.title: 72-Hour Home Survival Kit (Shelter at Home)
guides.quickAnswer: Store at least three days of water, food, light, radio, first aid, sanitation, and key documents in one clearly labeled home kit.
guides.whenToUse: Use before storms, earthquakes, or prolonged service disruptions when you may need to remain at home without safe water, power, or outside help.
preferredAction: Build one centralized kit sized for everyone in the household for at least 72 hours.
Keep it in a cool, dry, easy-to-reach location that all household members know.
backupAction: If you cannot fully stock 72 hours, start with water, flashlight, radio, medications, and basic food, then expand over time.
steps: Plan at least 1 gallon of water per person per day for 3 days.
Pack 3 days of shelf-stable, easy-to-eat food and a manual can opener.
Add flashlights, spare batteries, and a battery-powered or hand-crank radio.
Include first aid, prescription medications, and essential medical supplies.
Pack sanitation items, hygiene supplies, blankets, sturdy shoes, and copies of critical documents.
Add infant, child, pet, or special-needs items as required.
Review and refresh the kit every 6–12 months.
warnings: Utilities, tap water, and fuel may be unavailable after major disasters.
Temperature-sensitive medicines and devices need special planning during outages.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on cook-only foods that require large amounts of water or fuel.
Do not scatter supplies randomly around the house without one clearly marked core kit.
redFlags: Running out of life-sustaining medication, safe water, or basic shelter supplies during an active emergency needs urgent outside help or official support.
Chest pain, confusion, breathing trouble, or medication failure should be treated as urgent medical issues.
preparednessTips: Aim toward a 2-week home supply over time even if 72 hours is your baseline.
Keep a written inventory taped to or stored inside the kit.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Preparedness Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used household kit and go-bag guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit / Make A Plan materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core all-hazards preparedness checklists used nationwide.

guides.title: Personal Evacuation Go-Bag (Adult)
guides.quickAnswer: Keep a grab-and-go backpack with three days of essentials so you can leave quickly during fires, floods, chemical incidents, or fast evacuations.
guides.whenToUse: Use for immediate evacuations, short shelter stays, or situations where you may be away from home for several days.
preferredAction: Maintain one go-bag per adult near an exit and update it every 6–12 months.
backupAction: If no prepared go-bag exists, quickly gather water, ID, medications, cash, phone power, and a change of clothing if there is still time to do so safely.
steps: Choose a backpack you can carry comfortably for at least 15–20 minutes.
Pack water, compact shelf-stable snacks, a full change of clothes, and hygiene basics.
Include medications in original packaging, a small first-aid kit, and a written medication list.
Add a flashlight or headlamp, batteries, a whistle, and a small power bank.
Carry copies of ID, emergency contacts, and some cash in small bills.
Store the bag near an exit and practice grabbing it during drills.
warnings: Some emergencies give you only minutes to leave.
Smoke, rising water, or chemical odors can quickly make your home unsafe.
whatNotToDo: Do not make the bag so heavy that you cannot move quickly with it.
Do not pack only valuables while forgetting water, medications, and clothing.
redFlags: If officials order immediate evacuation, leave at once.
If smoke, floodwater, or hazardous fumes are already present, prioritize life safety over finishing your packing.
preparednessTips: Keep your vehicle fueled during high-risk seasons.
Add a laminated contact/medical card to the bag.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Preparedness Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used household kit and go-bag guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit / Make A Plan materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core all-hazards preparedness checklists used nationwide.

guides.title: Car / Work Mini Emergency Kit (24–72 Hours)
guides.quickAnswer: Keep a compact emergency kit in your car or workplace so you can manage delays, sheltering, or being stranded away from home.
guides.whenToUse: Use for commuting, remote driving, workplace lockdowns, severe weather travel, or short disruptions that keep you away from home.
preferredAction: Build a compact separate kit for your vehicle and another for your workplace with water, food, light, first aid, and basic tools.
backupAction: If you cannot keep a full kit, at least keep water, snacks, a flashlight, and a small first-aid kit available.
steps: Use a small bag or bin that fits in your trunk or under a desk.
Add bottled water, sealed snacks, and a basic first-aid kit.
Include a flashlight, spare batteries, and warmth items like a hat, gloves, socks, or emergency blanket where climate requires.
Pack a whistle, multitool, small duct tape, and a paper map.
Store printed key phone numbers and alternate route information.
Check food, water, and seasonal clothing twice a year.
warnings: Cars become dangerous in extreme heat or cold.
Heat can damage some stored items in vehicles.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on phone navigation and battery life.
Do not store dangerous heat-sensitive aerosol items in parked vehicles.
redFlags: If you are stranded in dangerous temperatures, injured, or your vehicle smells like fuel or is smoking, call for help early.
If you cannot safely remain with the vehicle, move only if conditions and official guidance support it.
preparednessTips: Keep at least half a tank of fuel during storm or wildfire seasons.
Leave a medical-condition note in the glove box if useful for responders.

sourceReferences.title: 72-Hour Kit / Preparedness guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common compact-kit structure for commuting and workplace use.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Kit Fact Sheets
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Helpful small-kit planning patterns for mobility.

guides.title: Kit Maintenance and Rotation Schedule
guides.quickAnswer: Emergency kits only work if you check and refresh them regularly. Replace expired food, water, batteries, and medications on a schedule.
guides.whenToUse: Use after creating kits and then every 6–12 months or before major risk seasons.
preferredAction: Set a recurring reminder and use a checklist to review every kit on a regular schedule.
backupAction: If you cannot do a full check, do a quick scan for expired food, dead batteries, low medications, and outdated documents.
steps: Schedule a kit review every 6–12 months.
Discard and replace expired or damaged food and water.
Check emergency medications and refill backup supplies when possible.
Test flashlights, radios, smoke alarms, and battery-powered devices.
Update IDs, contact lists, insurance copies, and family-specific items.
Relabel or repack kits with the latest review date.
warnings: Expired medications, swollen cans, dead batteries, and stale water can make a kit unreliable at the worst moment.
Home safety systems that fail can leave you without warning during fast-moving emergencies.
whatNotToDo: Do not build kits and forget them for years.
Do not use kit items casually unless you have a strict replace-after-use habit.
redFlags: If a disaster is underway and critical medicines or home warning systems are missing or broken, seek replacement or safe alternate shelter urgently.
If smoke alarms or carbon monoxide alarms fail and cannot be restored, consider temporary safer lodging where appropriate.
preparednessTips: Use first-in, first-out rotation for stored food.
Keep a printed maintenance log with dates and notes.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness Kit Maintenance
sourceReferences.organization: American Red Cross / municipal preparedness checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common schedule-based kit maintenance guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Smoke Alarm Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for integrating alert-system maintenance with kit maintenance.

guides.title: Basic Family Emergency Communication Plan
guides.quickAnswer: Write down who to contact, how to reach them, and where to meet if phones, travel, or routines are disrupted during an emergency.
guides.whenToUse: Use for all households, especially those with children, older adults, caregivers, or family members who spend the day in different places.
preferredAction: Create a simple printed family communication plan and give every household member a copy.
backupAction: If you cannot print, handwrite the plan and store photos of it on phones plus one paper copy in a visible home location.
steps: List names, mobile numbers, emails, and any key medical needs.
Choose one out-of-area contact who can relay updates.
Write down multiple contact methods such as text, calls, and email.
Record emergency numbers and local official hotlines.
Create wallet cards with emergency contacts and meeting points.
Store copies at home, in kits, and on phones.
Review annually or after any move or number change.
warnings: Phone networks may be overloaded; texts may work when calls do not.
A single app or single device is not enough for family communication resilience.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume everyone remembers phone numbers from memory.
Do not keep the only copy of the plan on one phone.
redFlags: If family members are unreachable during an active life-threatening incident, contact authorities.
If a dependent person with urgent medical needs loses caregiver contact, seek emergency help.
preparednessTips: Teach children how to call emergency services and state their name and address.
Set ICE contacts on phones.

sourceReferences.title: Family Emergency Communication Plan Templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard household planning format used broadly.

sourceReferences.title: Get a Kit, Make a Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family planning guidance with contacts and meeting points.

guides.title: Meeting Places and Reunion Planning
guides.quickAnswer: Choose and document meeting places near home, in the neighborhood, and outside your area so everyone knows where to go if separated.
guides.whenToUse: Use before disasters that might separate family members, such as fires, floods, wildfires, evacuations, or area-wide communication failures.
preferredAction: Define at least three meeting places and share them clearly with everyone in the household.
backupAction: If no prior plan exists, send one clear meeting point by text as soon as you become aware of a safe gathering location.
steps: Choose a near-home meeting point for small incidents like house fires.
Choose a neighborhood location in case you cannot return home.
Choose an out-of-area meeting point for major evacuations.
Write the addresses and when each point should be used.
Practice walking key routes with children and caregivers.
Review and update the locations when hazards, roads, or home address changes.
warnings: Some locations may become unsafe during specific disasters; review them with hazard context in mind.
Official shelter or evacuation instructions may override your default reunion locations.
whatNotToDo: Do not choose vague places that children or visitors may misunderstand.
Do not assume everyone will pick the same place without documenting it.
redFlags: If someone fails to arrive at a meeting point in known danger conditions, contact authorities.
If a child is missing near an active hazard, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Mark meeting places on a printed map kept in kits.
Share reunion information with babysitters, schools, and close neighbors.

sourceReferences.title: Family Emergency Communication Plan Templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard household planning format used broadly.

sourceReferences.title: Get a Kit, Make a Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family planning guidance with contacts and meeting points.

guides.title: School, Work, and Caregiver Coordination
guides.quickAnswer: Your family plan should fit the emergency procedures used by schools, workplaces, and caregivers—not conflict with them.
guides.whenToUse: When children attend school or childcare, adults work away from home, or anyone depends on outside caregivers or home-health support.
preferredAction: Collect written emergency procedures and contact details from every school, workplace, and caregiver involved in your household plan.
backupAction: If no formal plan is available, confirm by phone how the location handles lockdowns, evacuations, and reunification.
steps: For each school or childcare site, record contact numbers and pick-up rules.
For each workplace, note evacuation or shelter-in-place procedures.
List agencies, backup caregivers, and contact numbers for people with extra support needs.
Align your meeting places and pickup assumptions with those outside plans.
Store copies of these procedures with your family plan and go-bags.
Review them at the start of each school or work year.
warnings: Schools and workplaces may not release people early during some emergencies.
Unauthorized pickup may be refused.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume schools or employers will automatically notify your chosen out-of-area contact.
Do not leave caregivers without clear written emergency instructions.
redFlags: If you cannot locate a child or dependent person during an emergency, speak to site officials and call authorities if needed.
If a medically dependent person is stranded without care, contact emergency services.
preparednessTips: Save emergency hotline numbers for schools and workplaces in phones and on paper.
Review coordination details every year.

sourceReferences.title: State emergency family planning guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful coordination framework for families, schools, and caregivers.

sourceReferences.title: Municipal preparedness checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical pick-up and reunification planning ideas.

guides.title: Practice Drills and Plan Refresh
guides.quickAnswer: Plans work better when you rehearse them. Run simple drills for evacuation, meeting places, and communication, then update your plan based on what you learn.
guides.whenToUse: Use after creating or changing a family emergency plan and at least once a year thereafter.
preferredAction: Schedule simple age-appropriate drills for evacuation, check-ins, and shelter-in-place scenarios.
backupAction: If full drills are hard to run, do table-talk walk-throughs and scenario discussions instead.
steps: Practice a home evacuation and meeting-point drill.
Walk to neighborhood reunion points with children.
Test communication methods with your out-of-area contact.
Run a basic shelter-in-place practice in the chosen room.
After each drill, note what worked and what failed.
Update your written plan and kit contents accordingly.
warnings: Avoid causing panic or trauma during drills, especially for children or anxious household members.
Do not simulate dangerous conditions in ways that create real hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not skip drills because everyone 'already knows' the plan.
Do not practice only one scenario forever.
redFlags: If mobility or anxiety issues prevent safe evacuation practice, seek professional or local emergency-management guidance.
Severe distress during drills may justify health-professional support.
preparednessTips: Tie drills to time changes, school start, or storm seasons as reminders.
Let children occasionally help lead simple drills.

sourceReferences.title: USFA fire safety materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Drill and escape-practice principles.

sourceReferences.title: Family plan templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful annual-refresh structure for household plans.

guides.title: Shelter-in-Place Kit (Home)
guides.quickAnswer: Prepare a dedicated kit so your household can remain indoors safely for at least three days during storms, air-quality events, chemical releases, or security incidents.
guides.whenToUse: Use when authorities advise staying indoors rather than evacuating, or when travel is too dangerous and the household must remain inside.
preferredAction: Build a dedicated shelter-in-place kit that can be moved quickly into your chosen safe room.
backupAction: If no dedicated kit exists, gather your 72-hour home supplies into the room you will use for sheltering.
steps: Pack three days of water and food per person.
Add flashlights, batteries, and a radio.
Include sanitation items and simple waste-management supplies.
Keep plastic sheeting, duct tape, scissors, and towels for room sealing if official instructions call for it.
Store medications, first aid, pet supplies, and comfort items with the kit.
Identify the room where the kit would be used.
warnings: Chemical or smoke incidents may require sealing the room and turning off ventilation only if authorities tell you to do so.
Indoor generator use can cause fatal carbon monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave doors or windows open during a shelter-in-place order for smoke or chemical danger.
Do not rely only on candles for lighting.
redFlags: Breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, or loss of consciousness need emergency help.
If you smell strong gas or chemical odors inside, follow emergency instructions and evacuate if safe and advised.
preparednessTips: Choose an interior room with few windows in advance.
Keep a printed shelter-in-place steps sheet with the kit.

sourceReferences.title: Shelter-in-Place Checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common household room-sealing and supply guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness kit guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Good overlap with dedicated indoor shelter kits.

guides.title: Essential Medication Backup Planning
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guides.quickAnswer: Work with clinicians and pharmacists to maintain a backup supply of essential medicines and a clear medication list before disasters disrupt access.
guides.whenToUse: When anyone in the household depends on daily or life-sustaining prescription medicines, especially before storm, wildfire, flood, or outage seasons.
preferredAction: Create an emergency medication plan that includes backup supply, safe storage, rotation, and a current medication list.
backupAction: If you cannot get extra medicine, keep an up-to-date list of names, doses, prescribers, and pharmacies so care can be restored faster.
steps: Make a list of every essential medication, dose, schedule, doctor, and pharmacy.
Ask your doctor or pharmacist about keeping a 7–30 day buffer supply if possible.
Understand refill and insurance rules before disasters happen.
Store medicine in original labeled packaging in a cool, dry place.
Plan specifically for temperature-sensitive medicines.
Rotate older medicine first and monitor expiry dates.
Keep copies of the medication list in your wallet, kit, and with a trusted contact.
warnings: Stopping or changing critical medicines suddenly can be dangerous.
Improper storage can make some medicines unsafe or ineffective.
whatNotToDo: Do not mix pills into unlabeled containers.
Do not store medicines in overheated cars or direct sun.
redFlags: Running out of critical medication with symptoms such as chest pain, seizure activity, severe shortness of breath, or dangerous blood sugar problems needs urgent care.
Flood- or fire-contaminated medicine should not be used without professional guidance.
preparednessTips: Keep photos of prescription labels with your document kit.
Record medication allergies clearly.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency medication planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong principles for refill planning, documentation, and storage.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness for medicines
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports backup supply planning and medical readiness.

guides.title: Medical Information and Health Document Backup
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guides.quickAnswer: Keep copies of medical summaries, allergies, insurance information, and provider contacts in both paper and digital form so care can continue when systems are disrupted.
guides.whenToUse: Use for chronic illness, complex medical history, allergies, travel, evacuation, or any situation where records may be hard to access quickly.
preferredAction: Create a waterproof health document packet plus secure digital copies that can travel with you or your go-bag.
backupAction: If a full packet is hard to build, at least create a one-page medical summary with medicines, allergies, diagnoses, and emergency contacts.
steps: Include medication lists, allergies, diagnoses, surgeries, implants, and provider contacts.
Add insurance cards or numbers and emergency contact details.
Store copies in a waterproof folder with other critical documents.
Keep digital copies on a secure device or cloud account with safe password access.
Update the packet annually or after major medical changes.
warnings: Outdated medical info can cause mistakes.
Sensitive health information should be stored securely.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on one clinic's electronic record system.
Do not assume emergency providers will know your history without your help.
redFlags: If records are unavailable during a serious medical event, seek care immediately and communicate what you know.
If your health data is exposed or stolen, notify providers and insurers promptly.
preparednessTips: Keep a short medical summary for each family member in go-bags.
List preferred hospitals or clinics when useful.

sourceReferences.title: Health document backup guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Good basis for medical summary and record continuity.

guides.title: Household Critical Documents Kit
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guides.quickAnswer: Gather copies of IDs, insurance, legal, financial, property, and emergency documents into a protected grab-and-go kit before disaster damages the originals.
guides.whenToUse: When building home emergency kits and go-bags or before floods, wildfires, storms, or evacuations that could destroy or isolate original documents.
preferredAction: Create one protected paper-and-digital document kit that can be grabbed quickly during evacuation.
backupAction: If you cannot copy everything at once, start by listing key account numbers, contact numbers, IDs, and insurance details.
steps: Collect IDs, passports, birth certificates, and similar documents.
Add insurance policies, account details, wills, powers of attorney, and property records.
Include home and vehicle photos or inventories.
Store copies in a waterproof, fire-resistant folder and secure digital backup.
Keep the kit in an easy-to-reach but protected location.
Review after major life changes.
warnings: Leaving all originals in one vulnerable spot can lead to complete loss.
Sensitive documents can be misused if poorly protected.
whatNotToDo: Do not store the only copy in a damp basement without protection.
Do not share full document copies casually or through insecure channels.
redFlags: If identity documents are destroyed or stolen, start replacement and fraud-protection steps quickly.
If legal or medical decision documents are unavailable before urgent care, inform providers immediately.
preparednessTips: Keep a checklist on the folder cover so you know what should be inside.
Review annually.

sourceReferences.title: Household document kit guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used public guidance for critical document readiness.

guides.title: Power-Dependent Medical Devices Planning
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guides.quickAnswer: If someone depends on powered medical equipment, plan now for outages, backup batteries, alternate locations, and emergency contacts.
guides.whenToUse: When households rely on oxygen concentrators, CPAP/BiPAP, dialysis support, powered mobility devices, or similar equipment and outages are possible.
preferredAction: Create a written outage plan with your doctor, equipment provider, and utility company where available.
backupAction: If formal medical-priority utility registration is not available, prepare charged batteries and alternate powered locations ahead of time.
steps: List each power-dependent device and its battery runtime.
Ask clinicians and suppliers about outage procedures and transport needs.
Check whether your utility has a medical-priority registration process.
Arrange backup power or alternate safe locations.
Store device instructions and emergency numbers with your medical documents.
Practice moving critical devices safely for evacuation.
warnings: Indoor generator use causes fatal carbon monoxide risk.
Improvised unsafe wiring can create fire and shock hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume responders automatically know you rely on power-dependent equipment.
Do not leave all batteries uncharged or stored in flood-prone areas.
redFlags: If a critical device fails and no backup exists, call emergency services immediately.
If a person dependent on oxygen, ventilation, or dialysis is in distress, seek urgent care.
preparednessTips: Create a one-page device summary for responders.
Keep spare essential device parts when possible.

sourceReferences.title: Home medical equipment outage planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Consistent preparedness principles for device-dependent households.

guides.title: Pet Emergency Go-Kit
guides.quickAnswer: Prepare a pet kit with food, water, medications, records, and containment so your animal can evacuate or shelter with you.
guides.whenToUse: When any household has pets and may need to evacuate, shelter in place, or stay away from home for days or weeks during a disaster.
preferredAction: Keep a labeled pet emergency kit stored near your household go-bags.
backupAction: If you cannot prepare a full kit yet, at least keep ID, proof of vaccination, food, and a way to contain the pet ready.
steps: Store 2 weeks of pet food and safe water.
Pack bowls, a manual can opener if needed, medications, and written care instructions.
Include veterinary and vaccination records plus proof of ownership.
Keep collars, ID tags, microchip details, recent photos, and a carrier or crate ready.
Add sanitation and comfort items like litter, poop bags, toys, and bedding.
warnings: Shelters may not accept pets in all areas.
Stress can make animals escape or behave unpredictably during emergencies.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave pets behind if at all possible.
Do not assume pets can safely drink or eat from any available source.
redFlags: Difficulty breathing, seizures, persistent vomiting, or serious injury needs veterinary emergency help.
If pets must be left temporarily, notify animal services or authorities as soon as possible.
preparednessTips: Write clear feeding and medication instructions.
Keep carriers easy to reach, not buried in storage.

sourceReferences.title: Pet disaster preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong baseline kit and evacuation guidance for pets.

sourceReferences.title: Veterinary emergency preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Helpful detail on medicines, records, and transport.

guides.title: Pet Evacuation and Shelter Planning
guides.quickAnswer: Know where your pets can go before you need to evacuate. Confirm shelters, hotels, friends, or boarding options ahead of time.
guides.whenToUse: When building family evacuation plans or before hazards likely to trigger evacuation orders such as flood, wildfire, storm, or hazardous-material incidents.
preferredAction: Identify pet-friendly options and write them into your family emergency plan before a disaster happens.
backupAction: If you have no guaranteed option, still take your pet and kit to the nearest emergency shelter and ask for pet support or referral.
steps: Research local pet-friendly shelters and emergency animal services.
List relatives, friends, boarding options, and pet-friendly hotels.
Write down addresses, contacts, and any vaccination requirements.
Keep carriers and leashes accessible and practice loading pets calmly.
Bring the pet kit and records on every evacuation.
warnings: Some general shelters only allow service animals inside main areas.
Pets without vaccination records may be refused at some facilities.
whatNotToDo: Do not release pets outdoors and assume they will survive alone.
Do not show up with uncontained aggressive animals if you can prevent that safely.
redFlags: If you must leave pets temporarily, notify authorities or animal services as soon as possible.
Injured pets need veterinary emergency care.
preparednessTips: Include pet plans in the family communication and reunion plan.
Practice short travel sessions so pets tolerate carriers and vehicles better.

sourceReferences.title: Pet evacuation planning
sourceReferences.organization: CDC / local emergency pet safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful planning structure for shelters and transport.

guides.title: Home Fire Safety and Escape Readiness
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guides.quickAnswer: Install and test smoke alarms, keep extinguishers accessible, and practice two escape routes from every room with a family meeting point.
guides.whenToUse: For all households, especially multi-story homes or homes with children, older adults, or higher cooking/heating fire risk.
preferredAction: Use a written home fire safety checklist and practice an escape plan regularly.
backupAction: If you cannot do everything immediately, at least test alarms, clear exits, and choose a meeting place.
steps: Install smoke alarms on every level and near sleeping areas.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries as needed.
Replace alarms older than about 10 years.
Keep an ABC fire extinguisher on each floor if possible.
Identify two exits from each room and make sure they open easily.
Choose and practice an outside meeting place.
Keep exits clear of clutter.
warnings: Home fires spread fast and can leave only minutes to escape.
Once outside, re-entry is extremely dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not disable smoke alarms instead of fixing nuisance alarm causes.
Do not try to fight large or spreading fires instead of evacuating.
redFlags: If an alarm sounds and you see or smell smoke, evacuate and call emergency services.
If exits are blocked or mobility issues prevent escape, seek professional planning help before an emergency.
preparednessTips: Teach children to crawl low under smoke and never hide during a fire.
Use time-change dates or other regular reminders to test alarms.

sourceReferences.title: Smoke alarm and home escape planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: National fire-safety guidance for alarms and escape drills.

sourceReferences.title: Home hazard checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical room-by-room safety planning support.

guides.title: Utility Shutoff and Gas Leak Safety
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guides.quickAnswer: Learn where your gas, water, and electrical shutoffs are and what to do if you smell gas or see dangerous utility damage.
guides.whenToUse: Use after earthquakes, storms, leaks, flooding, or during emergency planning and household maintenance.
preferredAction: Locate and label shutoffs in advance, keep the right tools nearby, and evacuate rather than taking risks when you suspect a gas leak.
backupAction: If you are unsure how to shut something off safely, leave and call the utility or emergency services.
steps: Identify the gas, water, and electrical main shutoffs.
Keep a shutoff wrench where adults can find it.
If you smell gas or hear hissing, leave immediately without using switches, phones, or flames.
Turn off water for major leaks or contamination concerns.
Turn off the main breaker if flooding, fire, or obvious wiring damage is present and you can do so safely from a dry location.
Post utility instructions and contacts near the shutoffs.
warnings: Only qualified personnel should restore gas service after shutoff.
Standing in water around electricity is highly dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore gas smell or repeated electrical warning signs.
Do not store flammables near gas or electrical equipment.
redFlags: Strong gas odor, hissing, sparks, burning smell, or repeated breaker trips may need emergency help and utility response.
Symptoms like dizziness or nausea near suspected gas leaks warrant immediate evacuation and help.
preparednessTips: Practice identifying shutoffs with all responsible adults.
Keep utility contact numbers with your family emergency plan.

sourceReferences.title: Home utility and gas safety
sourceReferences.organization: Municipal home hazard / utility safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common household shutoff and leak-response principles.

guides.title: Basic Home Hazard and Injury Prevention Check
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guides.quickAnswer: Walk through your home to reduce the everyday fire, trip, electrical, and chemical hazards that become worse during disasters.
guides.whenToUse: When moving into a new home, before severe-weather seasons, or as an annual household safety check.
preferredAction: Use a room-by-room checklist to find and fix small hazards before they become emergency problems.
backupAction: If a full walkthrough is too much, start with bedrooms, exits, stairs, and the kitchen.
steps: Check for overloaded outlets and damaged cords.
Clear walkways and stairs and secure rugs.
Store flammables away from heat sources.
Lock chemicals away from children and separate them from food.
Secure heavy furniture and shelves in earthquake-prone areas.
Check exterior paths and make sure the house number is visible from the street.
warnings: Falls and fires are common home injuries that worsen under stress and poor lighting.
Small electrical issues can become major fire hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore recurring flickering lights, hot outlets, or trip hazards.
Do not store flammables near stoves, heaters, or candles.
redFlags: Frayed wiring, scorch marks, chemical exposure with breathing trouble, or dangerous structural instability need urgent attention and sometimes evacuation.
preparednessTips: Repeat the same checklist yearly so improvements are trackable.
Involve older children in basic hazard awareness.

sourceReferences.title: Home hazard checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common room-by-room injury and fire prevention practices.

guides.title: Alert Systems: Smoke Alarms, Weather Radio, and Phone Alerts
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guides.quickAnswer: Use layered alerts: working smoke alarms, a weather radio, and emergency phone alerts so one failure does not leave you without warning.
guides.whenToUse: When setting up home readiness, before severe-weather seasons, or when building a multi-channel warning system for the household.
preferredAction: Maintain smoke alarms, enable emergency phone alerts, and add a NOAA Weather Radio or equivalent official alert device where relevant.
backupAction: If you cannot get a weather radio right away, make sure smoke alarms work and phone alerts are enabled while following local radio or TV channels.
steps: Install smoke alarms on every level and in or near bedrooms.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries or units as needed.
Set up a weather radio with battery backup if available in your area.
Enable Wireless Emergency Alerts or the local equivalent on phones.
Teach the household what each alarm or alert means and how to respond.
Keep backup charging options for phones and radios.
warnings: One alert method can fail or be missed.
Turning off alerts for convenience can leave you without life-saving warning.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore emergency alerts assuming they are overblown.
Do not place radios where no one can hear them overnight.
redFlags: Any severe emergency alert such as tornado warning, evacuation order, or active smoke alarm with no clear cause requires immediate action or cautious investigation.
If alarms sound and you cannot identify the cause safely, call authorities.
preparednessTips: Test weather radios and alert settings regularly.
Keep a backup battery pack for phones.
